title: Management information systems
summary: Modern operations run on data as much as on machines. This HL-only lesson works through the whole of syllabus 5.9: how data and information systems feed business decision-making; big data and data analytics/data mining; artificial intelligence, machine learning and algorithms in operations and decisions; the role of databases, cloud computing and the internet of things; cybersecurity and cybercrime; data privacy, ethics and the critical/virtual perspectives on management information systems (MIS); and a balanced assessment of the benefits and risks of relying on MIS. Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation — so you can see why a supported judgement, not a memorised list, is what earns the top band.
learningObjectives: Distinguish data from information and explain the role of information systems in operational, tactical and strategic decision-making.
Explain big data (volume, velocity, variety) and how data analytics and data mining turn it into decisions.
Explain the role of artificial intelligence, machine learning and algorithms in operations and decisions, and of databases, cloud computing and the internet of things.
Explain cybersecurity, cybercrime, and the data-privacy, ethical and critical/virtual perspectives raised by MIS.
Evaluate the benefits and risks to a business of relying on MIS, applying the concepts to context and reaching a supported judgement.

simpleExplanation.title: From raw data to a better decision
simpleExplanation.summary: A management information system is the plumbing that carries raw facts from every corner of a business, cleans and organises them, and delivers meaningful information to the manager who has to decide something. The point is never the data itself — it is the sharper, faster, better-targeted decision the data makes possible, and the new risks that come with depending on it.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a supermarket checkout. Every beep of a barcode is a piece of raw data — a fact with no meaning on its own. On its own, '1,000 units sold' tells a manager nothing. Feed thousands of those beeps into a system that sorts them by product, store and hour, and out comes information: 'umbrella sales in the north stores jump 40% the day before rain.' The manager can now act — reorder stock, shift a promotion, move staff. Big data, analytics and AI simply do this at enormous scale and speed; cybersecurity and privacy are the locks and rules that keep all those beeps from being stolen or misused.
steps: Collect data from across the business and beyond — sales, stock, website clicks, sensors, social media — much of it flowing in continuously.
Store and organise it in databases, increasingly hosted on the cloud so it can be reached from anywhere and scaled up cheaply.
Process and analyse it. Data analytics and data mining find patterns; AI, machine learning and algorithms predict, recommend and even decide automatically.
Deliver information to the right manager as reports, dashboards or alerts — the raw material of a decision.
Act, and protect. Managers make operational, tactical and strategic decisions, while cybersecurity, privacy rules and ethics govern how the data is guarded and used.

flashcards.back: A system of people, processes and technology that collects, stores, processes and delivers data as meaningful information to help managers make decisions. It is a business system, not merely the IT department that runs the hardware.

Data are raw, unprocessed facts and figures (e.g. '1,000 units sold'). Information is data that has been processed, organised and given context so it means something to a decision-maker (e.g. 'sales rose 20% this quarter, driven by the north region').

Datasets so large, fast-moving and varied that ordinary tools cannot handle them. Commonly described by the 3Vs: volume (huge quantity), velocity (speed of generation and processing) and variety (structured and unstructured forms — text, images, video, sensor readings).

The process of examining datasets to uncover patterns, correlations and trends that inform decisions. It ranges from describing what happened to predicting what will happen and recommending what to do about it.

Digging through large databases to discover previously unknown patterns and relationships — for example finding that customers who buy one product tend also to buy another, so the two can be promoted together.

Computer systems that perform tasks normally requiring human intelligence — recognising images, understanding language, forecasting demand, making or recommending decisions. In business it powers chatbots, recommendation engines and automated quality checks.

A branch of AI in which a system improves its performance by learning patterns from data rather than being explicitly programmed with rules. The more data it sees, the better its predictions can become — for example a demand forecast that sharpens each season.

A defined set of step-by-step rules a computer follows to process data and reach an output — for example the rules that decide which product to recommend, which transaction to flag as fraud, or how to route a delivery.

An organised, structured store of data that can be searched, sorted and updated. Databases are the backbone of an MIS, holding customer, stock, sales and operational records in a form analytics can use.

Delivering computing services — storage, servers, databases, software, analytics — over the internet on demand, instead of owning on-site hardware. It lets a business scale capacity up or down and pay for what it uses.

Everyday physical objects — sensors, machines, vehicles, wearables — connected to the internet so they can send and receive data. In operations, IoT sensors feed real-time data on machines and stock into the MIS.

The practice of protecting systems, networks and data from digital attack, unauthorised access, theft or damage. Essential to any MIS, because the data it holds is valuable and a breach can be financially and reputationally severe.

Criminal activity carried out using computers or networks — hacking, phishing, ransomware, data theft and fraud. It is the threat cybersecurity defends against, and a live risk for any business relying on an MIS.

An individual's right to control how their personal data is collected, stored and used. Businesses must be transparent and comply with data-protection law (such as the EU's GDPR) or face legal penalties and lost trust.

flashcards.front: Trap: MIS is just the IT department
flashcards.back: False. IT provides the hardware, software and networks — the plumbing. An MIS is the wider business system that uses that plumbing, including the people, processes and data, to improve management decisions.

flashcards.front: Trap: more data always means better decisions
flashcards.back: Not necessarily. Poor-quality or biased data gives poor-quality decisions ('garbage in, garbage out'), and too much data can cause data overload and analysis paralysis. Relevance and quality beat sheer quantity.

quickCheck.prompt: A report states 'website sales rose 18% last month, led by mobile users in Germany.' In MIS terms, this statement is best described as:
options: Raw data, because it contains numbers
Information, because raw data has been processed and given context for a decision
Big data, because it comes from a website
An algorithm, because it was generated by a computer
quickCheck.answer: Information, because raw data has been processed and given context for a decision

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is the best example of a business using big data and data analytics?
options: Storing last year's invoices in a filing cabinet
Analysing millions of real-time clicks, purchases and reviews to predict which products to stock in each store
Emailing a single customer a receipt
Printing a weekly sales figure with no analysis
quickCheck.answer: Analysing millions of real-time clicks, purchases and reviews to predict which products to stock in each store

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is a cybersecurity risk that relying on an MIS creates for a business?
options: Faster access to customer information for managers
A ransomware attack that locks the firm's data until a payment is made
Lower long-run storage costs from using the cloud
The ability to personalise marketing to customers
quickCheck.answer: A ransomware attack that locks the firm's data until a payment is made

quickCheck.prompt: A manager argues that because an AI demand-forecasting tool 'processes so much data, it must be right.' Why is this reasoning flawed?
options: AI tools cannot process large datasets
If the data is poor-quality or biased, the output is unreliable ('garbage in, garbage out'), and over-reliance removes human judgement
AI is illegal to use in operations
Large datasets always guarantee accurate predictions
quickCheck.answer: If the data is poor-quality or biased, the output is unreliable ('garbage in, garbage out'), and over-reliance removes human judgement

quickCheck.prompt: Which pairing correctly separates a BENEFIT of analytics from a RISK of relying on MIS?
options: Benefit: a data breach exposing customers; Risk: better-targeted decisions
Benefit: better-targeted decisions and efficiency; Risk: data-privacy breaches and over-reliance on the system
Both listed items are risks
Both listed items are benefits
quickCheck.answer: Benefit: better-targeted decisions and efficiency; Risk: data-privacy breaches and over-reliance on the system

faq.q: What is the difference between an IT system and an MIS?
faq.a: An IT system is the technology itself — the hardware, software and networks. An MIS is the wider business system that puts that technology to work: it collects, processes and delivers information specifically to improve management decisions, and it includes people, processes and data as well as the technology. Think of IT as the plumbing and the MIS as the decision about how to use the water.

faq.q: How is machine learning different from a normal algorithm?
faq.a: A normal algorithm follows fixed rules a programmer wrote in advance — the same input always gives the same output. Machine learning is a type of AI that infers its own rules from data and improves as it sees more of it. A rules-based algorithm might flag any transaction over a set amount; a machine-learning fraud model learns the patterns of fraud from millions of past cases and updates itself as fraud tactics change.

faq.q: Is big data only relevant to giant companies like Amazon or Netflix?
faq.a: No. Large firms have the most sophisticated systems, but cloud computing and software-as-a-service have made powerful analytics affordable for small businesses too. A local café using its point-of-sale data to see which items sell at which times, or a boutique using social-media analytics to time a promotion, is using the same principles on a smaller scale.

faq.q: What is the 'critical' or 'virtual' perspective on MIS the syllabus mentions?
faq.a: It is the habit of questioning MIS rather than treating its outputs as neutral truth. The critical perspective asks who benefits and who is harmed — for example, does heavy employee surveillance or algorithmic decision-making disadvantage certain groups, and can biased data produce discriminatory outcomes in hiring or lending? The virtual perspective recognises that more of business now happens through data and online systems rather than physical interaction, which changes how trust, work and customer relationships operate. Both push you to weigh MIS critically instead of assuming the data is always right and always fair.

faq.q: How do I turn a 5.9 question into top-band marks?
faq.a: Do not just describe the technology. Name the concept (big data, analytics, AI, cybersecurity, data privacy), apply it to the specific business in the stimulus, weigh a genuine benefit against a genuine risk, and finish with a supported judgement. The marks live in the application and the balanced conclusion, not in a definition or a list of features.

sections.content: How does a streaming service seem to know what you want to watch next, or an online retailer suggest exactly the right product? Behind those decisions sits a management information system turning oceans of raw data into information a business can act on. Syllabus 5.9 is an HL-only topic about that process: how data and information systems support decisions; how big data, analytics and data mining find patterns; how artificial intelligence, machine learning and algorithms increasingly make or shape operational decisions; the role of databases, cloud computing and the internet of things; and the darker side — cybersecurity and cybercrime, data privacy, and the ethical and critical questions MIS raises. Because 5.9 is an evaluation-heavy topic, the worked examples below are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why a supported judgement outscores a memorised list every time.

The starting distinction in this topic is between data and information. Data are raw, unprocessed facts and figures — a sales figure, a click, a temperature reading — that mean little on their own. Information is data that has been processed, organised and given context so that a decision-maker can use it. A management information system (MIS) is the system of people, processes and technology that makes this transformation happen and delivers the result to managers. Crucially, an MIS is a business system, not simply the IT department: IT provides the hardware and software, but the MIS is about using that technology to improve decisions. Those decisions occur at three levels — operational (day-to-day, such as reordering stock), tactical (medium-term, such as planning a promotion) and strategic (long-term, such as entering a new market) — and good information improves all three.

items: **Data** are raw facts; **information** is processed, organised, contextualised data a manager can act on.
**An MIS** collects, stores, processes and delivers information — it combines technology with people and processes, and is broader than the IT department.
**Decision levels:** operational (routine), tactical (medium-term) and strategic (long-term) — an MIS supports all three.
**Garbage in, garbage out:** the value of the output depends on the quality and relevance of the input data, not just its quantity.

sections.content: Big data, data analytics and data mining

Modern systems increasingly work with big data — datasets so large, fast and varied that traditional tools cannot cope. Big data is often described by the 3Vs: volume (the sheer quantity), velocity (the speed at which it is generated and must be processed, often in real time) and variety (the different forms it takes, from structured database entries to unstructured text, images, video and sensor readings). Data on its own is useless; the value comes from data analytics, the process of examining datasets to uncover patterns, correlations and trends. Data mining is the related activity of digging through large databases to surface previously unknown relationships — for instance discovering which products tend to be bought together. Analytics can be descriptive (what happened), predictive (what is likely to happen) or prescriptive (what to do about it), and each step closer to prescription puts more decision-making power in the system's hands.

items: **The 3Vs of big data:** volume, velocity and variety — huge, fast-moving, mixed-format data beyond ordinary tools.
**Data analytics:** turns data into insight — descriptive (what happened), predictive (what will happen) and prescriptive (what to do).
**Data mining:** discovers hidden patterns and correlations in large databases, such as products frequently bought together.
**Business uses:** demand forecasting, inventory and route optimisation, market segmentation, personalisation, fraud detection and dynamic pricing.

sections.question: A high-street fashion retailer's MIS analyses sales data from its 50 stores and finds that sales of waterproof jackets rise by an average of 300% in any store on days the local forecast predicts more than 5mm of rain. The average profit per jacket is £25. The system can give a 48-hour advance warning of such a forecast, and the marketing team proposes sending a targeted promotion to loyalty members in the affected areas, costing £100 per store per event and expected to generate an extra 10 jacket sales per store. Calculate the net additional profit for a single store during one forecast event. [3]
sections.solution:
**Working.**
1. Additional profit from the extra sales:
   10 jackets × £25 profit per jacket = **£250**
2. Cost of the promotion:
   **£100** per store per event
3. Net additional profit:
   £250 − £100 = **£150**

**Answer:** the store gains a net additional profit of **£150** per forecast event.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 3 marks** — 'calculate' is an AO2/AO4 quantitative command, so the engine credits correct method and answer, not just the final number:

- **1 mark:** correct additional profit from extra sales (10 × £25 = £250).
- **1 mark:** correct deduction of the £100 promotion cost.
- **1 mark:** correct net figure of £150, clearly stated.

The wider point the engine expects you to grasp: the analytics linking weather data to sales data is only valuable because acting on it yields a positive net return — data becomes worthwhile when it changes a decision profitably.

sections.content: AI, machine learning and algorithms in operations and decisions

Artificial intelligence (AI) is the use of computer systems to perform tasks that would normally need human intelligence — recognising images, understanding language, forecasting demand or recommending decisions. Machine learning is the branch of AI in which a system learns patterns from data and improves with experience, rather than following rules a programmer fixed in advance; the more relevant data it sees, the sharper its predictions can become. An algorithm is the defined set of step-by-step rules a computer follows to turn an input into an output — the recommendation you receive, the delivery route chosen, the transaction flagged as fraud. In operations, these tools drive demand forecasting, predictive maintenance, quality control, warehouse robotics and dynamic pricing; in decision-making, they power recommendation engines, chatbots and automated approvals. The power is real, but so is the danger of treating an algorithm's output as neutral truth: an algorithm trained on biased data will reproduce that bias, and over-reliance can crowd out human judgement.

items: **AI:** systems performing tasks that normally need human intelligence — forecasting, recognition, language, decisions.
**Machine learning:** AI that learns from data and improves with experience, rather than following fixed rules.
**Algorithm:** the step-by-step rules a computer follows to produce an output (a recommendation, route, price or fraud flag).
**In operations:** demand forecasting, predictive maintenance, quality control, robotics, dynamic pricing, personalisation.
**The catch:** biased or poor data produces biased outputs, and over-reliance can displace human judgement — algorithms are not automatically neutral or correct.

sections.content: Databases, cloud computing and the internet of things

Underpinning all of this is the infrastructure that stores and moves the data. Databases are structured stores of data — customer, stock, sales and operational records — that can be searched, sorted and fed into analytics; they are the backbone of any MIS. Cloud computing delivers storage, servers, databases and software over the internet on demand, so a business can scale capacity up or down and pay for what it uses rather than buying and maintaining its own hardware — this has made powerful MIS tools affordable even for small firms. The internet of things (IoT) connects everyday physical objects — sensors, machines, vehicles, wearables — to the internet so they can send and receive data in real time. In operations, IoT sensors on machinery can feed the MIS live data that triggers predictive maintenance before a breakdown, or track stock automatically as it moves. Together, these technologies are what make big data and analytics practical at scale.

items: **Databases:** structured, searchable stores of data — the backbone that feeds an MIS.
**Cloud computing:** on-demand computing over the internet; scalable, pay-as-you-use, no large on-site hardware investment — makes MIS affordable for small firms.
**Internet of things (IoT):** connected physical objects and sensors sending real-time data — e.g. machine sensors enabling predictive maintenance or automatic stock tracking.
**Together** they make big data and analytics practical, but they also widen the surface that must be secured.

sections.content: The more a business depends on data, the more it has to lose if that data is stolen, corrupted or locked. Cybercrime — hacking, phishing, ransomware, data theft and fraud — is a live threat to any firm running an MIS, and cybersecurity is the practice of protecting systems, networks and data against it. A single breach can be financially devastating (fines, remediation, lost sales) and reputationally worse: customers who no longer trust a business with their data take their custom elsewhere. Alongside security sits data privacy — the right of individuals to control how their personal data is collected and used. Businesses must be transparent about what they gather and comply with data-protection law such as the EU's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), or face heavy penalties. Cloud storage and the internet of things multiply the value an MIS creates, but they also widen the attack surface, so security and privacy are not optional extras — they are conditions of relying on an MIS at all.

items: **Cybercrime:** hacking, phishing, ransomware, data theft and fraud — the threats an MIS is exposed to.
**Cybersecurity:** protecting systems, networks and data from attack; a breach brings financial cost and lasting reputational damage.
**Data privacy:** individuals' right to control their personal data; firms must be transparent and comply with laws such as GDPR.
**Wider attack surface:** cloud and IoT increase both the value and the vulnerability of the data an MIS holds.

sections.content: Ethics and the critical / virtual perspectives

Beyond legality, MIS raises genuine ethical dilemmas, and the syllabus asks you to view them critically rather than accept the technology as neutral. Collecting vast personal data creates privacy concerns even where it is lawful. Employee monitoring can raise productivity but, taken too far, erodes trust and morale. Algorithms trained on biased data can produce discriminatory outcomes in hiring, lending or pricing — the critical perspective asks who benefits from an MIS and who may be harmed by it, refusing to treat the system's outputs as objective truth. The virtual perspective recognises that more business now happens through data and online systems than through physical interaction, reshaping how trust, work and customer relationships operate. A responsible business does not simply ask 'can we?' but 'should we?', building transparency, fairness and accountability into how it uses its MIS.

items: **Data privacy ethics:** collecting large amounts of personal data can be intrusive even when legal — transparency matters.
**Employee monitoring:** productivity tracking must be balanced against trust and morale.
**Algorithmic bias:** systems trained on biased data can discriminate — the critical perspective asks who benefits and who is harmed.
**Virtual perspective:** as business shifts into data and online systems, trust, work and relationships are reshaped.
**'Should we?' not just 'can we?':** responsible use builds in transparency, fairness and accountability.

sections.content: For an 'evaluate' or 'discuss' question on MIS, use the CUEGIS-style habit of weighing both sides in context. An MIS is a form of innovation that can drive strategy and efficiency, but its adoption is a change that raises ethical and security challenges. Do not simply describe what big data or AI is — name the concept, apply it to the specific business in the stimulus, weigh a real benefit against a real risk, and commit to a supported judgement. The application and the judgement are where the top-band marks are won.
The benefits and risks of relying on MIS

Relying on an MIS offers powerful benefits: faster and better-informed decisions, sharper demand forecasting and inventory control, personalised marketing that lifts sales, efficiency gains from automation, and a competitive advantage from understanding markets and customers more deeply than rivals. But dependence carries real risks. The upfront cost of systems, integration and training is high; poor-quality or biased data produces poor decisions; managers can suffer data overload and analysis paralysis; cybercrime and data-privacy breaches threaten finances and reputation; and over-reliance can erode human judgement, leaving a business exposed if the system is wrong or goes down. The examinable skill is not to memorise these lists but to weigh them for a specific business and decide when the benefits outweigh the risks — and when they do not.

sections.question: Discuss the benefits and risks to a business of using big data and data analytics in its decision-making. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Big data and data analytics can substantially improve the quality of a business's decisions. By analysing very large, fast-moving datasets — sales, website clicks, customer reviews and social-media activity — a business can uncover patterns it could never see manually and act on them. An online retailer, for example, can use predictive analytics to forecast demand for each product in each region, so it stocks the right goods in the right place and cuts both stock-outs and costly overstock. This directly improves operational efficiency and working-capital use, and the better-targeted decisions can raise sales.

Analytics also enables personalisation, which is a source of competitive advantage. By mining customer data, the business can recommend products each shopper is likely to want and time promotions precisely, lifting conversion rates and customer loyalty. A rival that decides on gut feeling is at a real disadvantage against one that decides on evidence, so in a competitive market analytics can be the difference between growth and decline.

However, relying on big data carries significant risks. First, cost: gathering, storing and analysing data at scale requires investment in systems, cloud infrastructure and skilled analysts, and for a smaller business this can be a heavy fixed cost that only pays off if the insights genuinely change decisions. Second, data quality and bias: analytics is only as good as its inputs — 'garbage in, garbage out' — and an algorithm trained on biased or unrepresentative data can produce misleading or even discriminatory conclusions that damage decisions and reputation. Third, privacy, ethics and cybersecurity: collecting large volumes of personal data raises privacy concerns and legal obligations under laws such as GDPR, and the more valuable data a firm holds, the more attractive a target it becomes for cybercrime — a single breach can bring fines and lasting loss of customer trust. Fourth, over-reliance: if managers treat analytics as infallible, they may stop exercising human judgement and be badly exposed when the data is wrong or the system fails.

**Judgement.** On balance, using big data and data analytics is likely to benefit most businesses, but only where it is used well and the risks are actively managed. The decisive factor is whether the insight actually changes decisions profitably: for a data-rich business in a competitive market — such as online retail — the gains in forecasting accuracy, efficiency and personalisation are substantial and hard to match without analytics, so the benefits usually outweigh the costs. But this holds only if the business invests in data quality, protects the data against cybercrime and uses it ethically and lawfully, and if managers keep human judgement in the loop rather than deferring blindly to the algorithm. For a small firm with little data and tight finances, or one that cannot secure the data properly, the balance can tip the other way. So the honest conclusion is conditional: big data and analytics are a powerful advantage when the value of the insight exceeds the cost and the privacy, security and over-reliance risks are controlled — and a liability when they are not.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1 — knowledge (relevant concepts):** the engine credits accurate command of the ideas the question turns on — big data (volume, velocity, variety), data analytics and data mining, algorithms and bias, cybersecurity and data privacy, over-reliance. Correct terminology signals AO1.
- **AO2 — application to a business:** marks are awarded for tying those concepts to a real context — e.g. an online retailer forecasting regional demand, personalising recommendations, or facing GDPR obligations and breach risk. Generic points that never touch a specific business do not earn AO2. (In the exam, apply to the business in the stimulus.)
- **AO3 — analysis and balanced evaluation:** the engine credits developed two-sided reasoning — benefits (better-targeted decisions, efficiency, personalisation, competitive advantage) weighed against risks (cost, data quality/bias, privacy and cybersecurity, over-reliance) — and, crucially, a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that decides when the benefits outweigh the risks and justifies WHY.
- **What lifts an answer into the top band:** application plus a supported judgement. A response that lists benefits and risks but never applies them and never decides is held in the lower bands, however long it is. A conditional judgement ('beneficial IF the value of the insight exceeds the cost and the risks are managed') is exactly the kind of reasoned conclusion the engine rewards.
- **What the engine rejects:** all-benefits or all-risks answers with no balance; a 'judgement' that merely restates both sides without choosing; and correct definitions with no link to a business decision.

items: **Confusing data with information** — data are raw facts; information is processed, contextualised data a manager can act on. Calling a raw figure 'information', or vice versa, signals a shaky grasp of the topic.
**Treating the MIS as just the IT department** — IT is the technology; the MIS is the wider business system (people, processes and data) that uses it to improve decisions.

**Assuming more data always means better decisions** — poor-quality or biased data gives poor decisions ('garbage in, garbage out'), and data overload can cause analysis paralysis. Quality and relevance beat quantity.

**Treating algorithms and AI as neutral or infallible** — a system trained on biased data reproduces that bias, and over-reliance removes human judgement. The critical perspective asks who benefits and who is harmed.
**Ignoring cybersecurity and privacy** — the more data a firm relies on, the greater the cybercrime and data-privacy risk; leaving these out of an evaluation misses half the picture.
**Describing the technology instead of applying it** — defining big data or AI earns AO1 only; the marks climb when each point is tied to the specific business in the case.
**Answering 'discuss' or 'evaluate' with no supported judgement** — giving both sides but never committing to a justified conclusion cannot reach the top band.

sections.content: MIS ties together much of the operations unit and the wider course. The forecasting and quality tools here feed directly into production planning, lean production and quality management; the cost-and-benefit reasoning connects to investment appraisal and break-even; and the ethics, privacy and stakeholder questions echo the corporate-governance and business-ethics threads that run throughout Business Management. Master the habit built here — identify the concept, apply it to the specific business, weigh benefits against risks, then commit to a supported judgement — and you have the template that earns marks across every evaluation question in the course.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: discuss the benefits and risks to a business of relying on MIS, applying the concepts and reaching a supported judgement
